summary: Markets are gripped by a deeply risk-off mood as an active U.S.-Iran conflict, including confirmed American casualties and CIA facility strikes with suspected Russian involvement, dominates investor sentiment and drives sharp moves across asset classes. The primary macro catalysts are the sharp deterioration in Middle East security, with Hormuz vessel crossings falling materially, Red Sea shipping disruptions intensifying, and President Trump threatening direct strikes on Iranian infrastructure including bridges and power plants in retaliation for any further targeting of ships. On the sector and stock front, energy names are under pressure from refining margin uncertainty even as crude prices rise on supply fears, the Indian rupee has weakened to two-month lows on oil cost concerns, J&J received regulatory approval for its robotic surgical system providing a rare bright spot in healthcare earnings, and Amazon's job cuts in its AGI unit signal selective cost discipline in AI spending. The dominant risk factors commanding investor attention are a potential World Bank-flagged scenario of global growth collapsing to just 1.3% in 2026 if the conflict escalates further, the EU expanding no-fly zones to Jordan, a Senate bill that could bar Mercedes-Benz and other foreign automakers with Chinese supply chain ties from the U.S. market, and the operational strain on an already stretched U.S. military. The short-term outlook remains highly volatile and headline-driven, with energy prices, shipping stocks, and defense names likely to see continued sharp swings as geopolitical developments unfold faster than economic data can anchor investor positioning.
